attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with concerns about her right arm. She underwent a right mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection for breast cancer two years ago, followed by radiotherapy. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes, for which she takes perindopril and metformin. She works as a teacher and lives with her husband.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her right arm shows no visible swelling compared to the left, but she reports intermittent heaviness and aching, particularly at the end of the day, which resolves by morning. There is no pitting oedema, but the skin feels slightly firmer on palpation compared to the contralateral arm. Range of motion is preserved.

Imaging reveals no malignancy recurrence or DVT.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Recommend compression garments to be worn as much as possible
Prescribe a low dose diuretic to reduce fluid retention
Reassure that these are normal post-surgical symptoms
Refer for formal lymphoedema assessment and education
Advise to elevate the arm when possible and engage in resistance training
attributes.explanation:
This patient is presenting with early warning signs of lymphoedema following breast cancer treatment involving axillary lymph node dissection and radiotherapy. The symptoms of heaviness, aching, and transient swelling that resolves overnight are classic early indicators of lymphatic dysfunction, even in the absence of visible oedema. The slightly firmer skin texture suggests early tissue changes. Excluding cancer recurrence, DVT and infection should proceed this diagnosis in a patient who has had breast cancer.

Refer for lymphoedema assessment and education is the most appropriate management. Early intervention is crucial to prevent progression to irreversible lymphoedema. A lymphoedema specialist can provide comprehensive assessment, education about risk reduction strategies, and appropriate exercises to promote lymphatic drainage. Studies show that early intervention can significantly reduce the risk of progression to chronic lymphoedema.

Reassure that these are normal post-surgical symptoms would be inappropriate as these symptoms represent early lymphoedema rather than normal post-surgical changes, especially two years after surgery. Dismissing these symptoms could lead to delayed intervention and progression to more advanced lymphoedema with fibrotic changes.

Prescribe a diuretic to reduce fluid retention is not appropriate for lymphoedema management. Diuretics are ineffective for lymphatic fluid accumulation and may lead to electrolyte imbalances and dehydration. They do not address the underlying pathophysiology of impaired lymphatic drainage.

Recommend compression garments to be worn continuously without proper assessment is premature. While compression therapy is a cornerstone of lymphoedema management, garments must be professionally fitted based on measurements and the stage of lymphoedema. Inappropriate compression can cause harm or discomfort.

Advise to elevate the arm whenever possible alone is insufficient management. While elevation may temporarily reduce swelling, it does not address the underlying lymphatic dysfunction. Comprehensive management requires a multimodal approach including exercise, skin care, manual lymphatic drainage, and possibly compression.

Lymphoedema risk factors include cancer treatments affecting lymph nodes (surgery, radiation), obesity, recurrent infections, and venous insufficiency. The condition progresses from Stage 0 (subclinical) with symptoms but no visible swelling, to Stage 1 (reversible) with pitting oedema that resolves with elevation, to Stage 2 (spontaneously irreversible) with fibrosis and non-pitting oedema, and finally Stage 3 (lymphostatic elephantiasis) with severe skin changes and disability.
learningPoints: Early warning signs of lymphoedema include heaviness, aching, swelling that resolves overnight, and skin changes in the affected limb.
Lymphoedema risk factors include cancer treatment affecting lymph nodes, obesity, and recurrent cellulitis.
Early intervention and specialised assessment of lymphoedema can prevent progression to irreversible fibrotic changes.
references: Lymphoedema Association Australia. Lymphoedema: Guide for diagnosis and management in general practice [Internet]. RACGP. 2018 [cited 2025 May 25]. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/getmedia/e20d45c8-000f-4e21-b52b-1cfe64ed7bb0/Lymphoedema-Guide.pdf.aspx
